era_context: The year 1840 falls in the early Victorian era and a period of aggressive European imperial expansion. Britain is the world's foremost industrial and naval power, projecting force into China, the Pacific, and the Middle East simultaneously. The Ottoman Empire pursues Tanzimat reforms while facing an existential military challenge from its vassal Muhammad Ali of Egypt. In Europe, nationalist and liberal movements are building toward the upheavals of 1848. The United States is consumed by westward expansion, the politics of slavery, and the aftermath of the Panic of 1837. Qing China faces its first major confrontation with a Western industrial power in the Opium War.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa outside European contact zones and the Zanzibar trade network
Central Asia and Inner Asian khanates
Southeast Asian mainland kingdoms (Burma, Siam, Vietnam, Cambodia) — events occurred but are underrepresented in English-language training data
Pacific Islands beyond New Zealand and Hawaii
Japanese domestic affairs under Tokugawa seclusion
Internal affairs of pre-colonial West African states

events.title: United States presidential election — William Henry Harrison wins
events.description: In the November 1840 presidential election, Whig candidate William Henry Harrison defeats incumbent Democrat Martin Van Buren in a landslide, winning 234 electoral votes to Van Buren's 60. The campaign, famous for its 'Tippecanoe and Tyler Too' slogan and populist log-cabin-and-hard-cider imagery, is one of the first modern American elections characterised by mass rallies, merchandising, and voter mobilisation. Van Buren is widely blamed for the Panic of 1837 and subsequent economic depression. John Tyler of Virginia serves as Harrison's running mate. Voter turnout reaches approximately 80%, a dramatic increase reflecting the expansion of white male suffrage and the professionalisation of party machinery.
key_figures: William Henry Harrison (Whig presidential candidate)
Martin Van Buren (incumbent President)
John Tyler (Whig vice-presidential candidate)
Thurlow Weed (Whig political organiser)

sources.name: Official election returns; Congressional records of the 26th Congress
Contemporary newspaper coverage (Niles' Weekly Register, New York Herald)
Robert Gray Gunderson, The Log-Cabin Campaign (1957)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in official records, newspapers, and personal correspondence. No dating uncertainty.

events.title: Treaty of Waitangi signed — British sovereignty over New Zealand
events.description: On 6 February 1840, Captain William Hobson and approximately 40 Māori rangatira (chiefs) sign the Treaty of Waitangi at Waitangi in the Bay of Islands. The treaty is subsequently carried around New Zealand and eventually signed by over 500 chiefs, though some prominent leaders refuse. The English and Māori texts diverge critically: the English version cedes 'sovereignty' to the British Crown, while the Māori text cedes 'kāwanatanga' (governance) — a concept that did not equate to full sovereignty in Māori political understanding. The Māori text guaranteed 'tino rangatiratanga' (full chieftainship) over lands, forests, and treasures. This foundational ambiguity would fuel legal and political disputes for the next two centuries. New Zealand is proclaimed a British colony in May 1840.
key_figures: Captain William Hobson (Lieutenant-Governor and British representative)
Hone Heke (prominent Ngāpuhi chief, first signatory)
Tāmati Wāka Nene (Ngāpuhi chief, spoke in favour of the treaty)
Henry Williams (Church Missionary Society, translator of the Māori text)
Te Wherowhero (Waikato chief, did not sign)

sources.name: Treaty of Waitangi, English and Māori texts (6 February 1840)
Despatches between Hobson and the Colonial Office (CO 209 series, The National Archives, UK)
Claudia Orange, The Treaty of Waitangi (1987)
Waitangi Tribunal reports (Te Reo Māori Report 1986; Muriwhenua Land Report 1997)

events.certainty_note: The signing is thoroughly documented. The content and meaning of the two texts are well-established; the dispute concerns interpretation, not the historical event itself.

events.title: Queen Victoria marries Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha
events.description: On 10 February 1840, Queen Victoria marries Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha at the Chapel Royal, St James's Palace, London. The marriage is a genuine love match — Victoria proposed to Albert herself, as protocol required since she outranked him. Albert would become Victoria's chief political adviser and a driving force behind the modernisation of the British monarchy, the patronage of science and industry (most notably the Great Exhibition of 1851), and reforms to royal household management. The marriage produces nine children whose marriages into European royal families earn Victoria the epithet 'grandmother of Europe,' shaping dynastic politics for generations.
key_figures: Queen Victoria (Queen of the United Kingdom)
Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha
Lord Melbourne (Prime Minister, Victoria's political mentor)

sources.name: Queen Victoria's journal entries, January–February 1840 (Royal Archives)
The Times, London, reports of the royal wedding, February 1840
A.N. Wilson, Victoria: A Life (2014)

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented in contemporary court records, newspapers, and Victoria's own extensive journals.

events.title: Penny Black — world's first adhesive postage stamp issued
events.description: On 1 May 1840, the Penny Black postage stamp goes on sale in the United Kingdom, becoming the world's first adhesive postage stamp used in a public postal system. It is the culmination of Rowland Hill's postal reform, which introduced uniform penny postage regardless of distance within Britain (effective 10 January 1840). The reform transforms communications: letter volume in Britain roughly doubles within a year and continues to grow exponentially. The concept of prepaid adhesive stamps is rapidly adopted by other countries — Zurich and Brazil follow in 1843. The reform democratises written communication, previously prohibitively expensive for the working class, and underpins the growth of commercial correspondence in the industrial age.
key_figures: Rowland Hill (postal reformer)
Henry Cole (civil servant, campaigner for reform)
Queen Victoria (depicted on the stamp)

sources.name: Post Office records, 1840; Rowland Hill's Post Office Reform: Its Importance and Practicability (1837)
Parliamentary debates and reports on postal reform, 1839–1840 (Hansard)
Catherine J. Golden, Posting It: The Victorian Revolution in Letter Writing (2009)

events.certainty_note: Precisely dated from Post Office records and surviving stamps. No uncertainty.

events.title: First Opium War — British expeditionary force arrives in China, captures Chusan
events.description: Following the declaration of hostilities in late 1839, a British expeditionary force of approximately 4,000 troops and 40 naval vessels arrives off the Chinese coast in June 1840. The force bypasses Canton and sails north, capturing the island of Chusan (Zhoushan) on 5–6 July with minimal resistance, shocking the Qing court. British forces then proceed further north to the Baihe river mouth near Tianjin, threatening Beijing itself. The Daoguang Emperor, alarmed, sends Qishan to negotiate, leading to the disgraced replacement of Commissioner Lin Zexu, whose destruction of British opium stocks in 1839 had precipitated the war. The campaign demonstrates the devastating technological and organisational gap between British industrial-age military forces and Qing defences.
key_figures: Rear Admiral George Elliot (joint British plenipotentiary)
Captain Charles Elliot (joint British plenipotentiary)
Lin Zexu (Qing Imperial Commissioner, dismissed)
Qishan (replacement Qing negotiator)
Daoguang Emperor (Qing Emperor)
Lord Palmerston (British Foreign Secretary)

sources.name: British Parliamentary Papers relating to China, 1840; despatches from Elliot to Palmerston
Qing imperial court records (Chouban Yiwu Shimo — documents on barbarian affairs)
Julia Lovell, The Opium War: Drugs, Dreams, and the Making of Modern China (2011)
Peter Ward Fay, The Opium War, 1840–1842 (1975)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented in both British and Chinese primary sources. Military operations precisely dated.

events.title: Convention of London resolves the Egyptian–Ottoman Crisis
events.region: Ottoman Empire / Egypt / European great powers
events.description: On 15 July 1840, Britain, Austria, Prussia, and Russia sign the Convention of London with the Ottoman Empire, demanding that Muhammad Ali of Egypt withdraw from Syria, Crete, and the Hejaz, retaining only hereditary rule over Egypt. France, which had supported Muhammad Ali, is excluded and humiliated. When Muhammad Ali refuses the terms, British and Ottoman forces bombard Beirut in September and Acre in November, and support a Lebanese uprising against Egyptian occupation. Muhammad Ali is compelled to accept the convention's terms by late 1840. The crisis demonstrates that no single European power can unilaterally alter the Ottoman balance of power, and France's isolation nearly triggers a wider European war before King Louis-Philippe's government backs down.
key_figures: Lord Palmerston (British Foreign Secretary, architect of the convention)
Muhammad Ali (Wali of Egypt)
Sultan Abdülmecid I (Ottoman Sultan)
Adolphe Thiers (French Prime Minister, dismissed over the crisis)
Prince Metternich (Austrian Chancellor)
Emir Bashir II (Lebanese emir, allied with Egypt)

sources.name: Text of the Convention of London, 15 July 1840
British Foreign Office despatches (FO 78 series, The National Archives, UK)
Khaled Fahmy, All the Pasha's Men: Mehmed Ali, His Army and the Making of Modern Egypt (1997)
M.S. Anderson, The Eastern Question 1774–1923 (1966)

events.certainty_note: Diplomatic documents fully preserved in multiple national archives. No dating uncertainty.

events.title: Act of Union unites Upper and Lower Canada
events.description: On 23 July 1840, the British Parliament passes the Act of Union 1840 (effective 10 February 1841), merging Upper Canada (predominantly English-speaking) and Lower Canada (predominantly French-speaking) into the single Province of Canada. The act is a direct response to Lord Durham's 1839 report, which recommended union as a means to assimilate French Canadians into English culture following the Rebellions of 1837–1838. The act establishes a single legislature with equal representation for both sections despite Lower Canada's larger population, makes English the sole official language of government (a provision later relaxed), and merges the two provinces' debts — effectively forcing the more fiscally sound Lower Canada to absorb Upper Canada's larger deficit. The act is deeply resented by French Canadians and shapes the political dynamics that eventually lead to Confederation in 1867.
key_figures: Lord Durham (author of the Durham Report)
Lord Sydenham (Charles Poulett Thomson, Governor General)
Louis-Hippolyte Lafontaine (French Canadian political leader)
Robert Baldwin (Reform leader, Upper Canada)

sources.name: Act of Union 1840 (3 & 4 Victoria, c. 35), UK Parliamentary records
Lord Durham's Report on the Affairs of British North America (1839)
J.M.S. Careless, The Union of the Canadas: The Growth of Canadian Institutions, 1841–1857 (1967)

events.certainty_note: Statutory legislation with exact date of passage. No uncertainty.

events.title: World Anti-Slavery Convention in London
events.description: In June 1840, the British and Foreign Anti-Slavery Society convenes the World Anti-Slavery Convention at Freemasons' Hall, London, bringing together over 500 delegates from across the transatlantic abolitionist movement. The convention becomes notable for its controversial refusal to seat female delegates, including the American abolitionists Lucretia Mott and Elizabeth Cady Stanton, who are confined to a screened-off gallery. This exclusion galvanises Mott and Stanton to organise what will become the women's rights movement, culminating in the Seneca Falls Convention of 1848. The convention itself debates strategies for ending slavery worldwide, particularly targeting the remaining slave-holding powers including the United States, Brazil, Cuba, and various African and Islamic states.
key_figures: Thomas Clarkson (veteran abolitionist, convention president)
Lucretia Mott (American abolitionist and women's rights advocate)
Elizabeth Cady Stanton (American women's rights advocate)
William Lloyd Garrison (American abolitionist, sat in the gallery in solidarity with excluded women)
Daniel O'Connell (Irish politician, speaker)

sources.name: Proceedings of the General Anti-Slavery Convention, London, 1840 (published by the BFASS)
Benjamin Robert Haydon, painting The Anti-Slavery Society Convention, 1840 (National Portrait Gallery)
Clare Midgley, Women Against Slavery: The British Campaigns, 1780–1870 (1992)

events.certainty_note: Official proceedings published; extensively documented in delegate correspondence and Haydon's painting. No dating uncertainty.

events.title: Independent Treasury Act signed into law in the United States
events.description: On 4 July 1840, President Martin Van Buren signs the Independent Treasury Act, establishing a system of sub-treasuries to hold government funds independently of private banks. The act is a direct response to the Panic of 1837, which demonstrated the dangers of depositing federal funds in state-chartered 'pet banks' (as Andrew Jackson had done after destroying the Second Bank of the United States). The Independent Treasury system requires all government payments to be made in gold or silver, reinforcing hard-money policy. The act is repealed by the Whigs in 1841 but re-established in 1846, and the sub-treasury system remains the basis of federal fiscal management until the creation of the Federal Reserve in 1913.
key_figures: Martin Van Buren (President of the United States)
Andrew Jackson (former President, whose bank war precipitated the issue)
Henry Clay (Whig opponent of the act)

sources.name: Independent Treasury Act, 4 July 1840 (U.S. Statutes at Large)
Congressional Globe, 26th Congress debates
David Kinley, The History, Organization and Influence of the Independent Treasury of the United States (1893)

events.certainty_note: Federal legislation with exact signing date. No uncertainty.

events.title: Frederick William IV becomes King of Prussia
events.description: On 7 June 1840, Frederick William IV succeeds his father Frederick William III as King of Prussia. Initially, liberals across the German states hope the new king — known for his romantic, intellectual temperament and apparent sympathy for reform — will grant a constitution and advance German unification. These hopes prove largely illusory: Frederick William IV is deeply committed to divine-right monarchy and refuses to accept the 'crown from the gutter' when offered the German imperial crown by the Frankfurt Parliament in 1849. His reign encompasses the upheavals of 1848, Prussia's growing rivalry with Austria for leadership of the German states, and the early career of Otto von Bismarck.
key_figures: Frederick William IV (King of Prussia)
Frederick William III (predecessor)
Alexander von Humboldt (scientist, influential at the Prussian court)

sources.name: Prussian court records and contemporary Berlin newspaper reports, June 1840
David E. Barclay, Frederick William IV and the Prussian Monarchy, 1840–1861 (1995)

events.certainty_note: Royal succession precisely documented. No uncertainty.

events.title: Louis-Napoléon's failed coup attempt at Boulogne
events.description: On 6 August 1840, Louis-Napoléon Bonaparte (nephew of Napoleon I) lands at Boulogne-sur-Mer with approximately 50 followers in a second attempt to seize power in France, following a similarly abortive attempt at Strasbourg in 1836. The coup is farcical — the local garrison refuses to join, and Louis-Napoléon is arrested, tried by the Chamber of Peers, and sentenced to life imprisonment at the fortress of Ham. He escapes in 1846 and, remarkably, is elected President of the French Republic in December 1848 before making himself Emperor Napoleon III in 1852. The Boulogne attempt, though a failure, keeps the Napoleonic legend alive in French politics and demonstrates the persistent appeal of Bonapartism among segments of the French population.
key_figures: Louis-Napoléon Bonaparte (Bonapartist pretender, future Napoleon III)
King Louis-Philippe (Orleanist King of the French)

sources.name: Trial records of the Chamber of Peers, October 1840
Contemporary French press coverage (Le Moniteur Universel, Le Constitutionnel)
Fenton Bresler, Napoleon III: A Life (1999)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in court records and contemporary press. No dating uncertainty.

events.title: Return of Napoleon's remains to Paris (Retour des Cendres)
events.description: On 15 December 1840, the remains of Napoleon I are ceremonially interred at Les Invalides in Paris, having been exhumed from his grave on Saint Helena and transported to France aboard the frigate Belle-Poule. The repatriation, authorised by King Louis-Philippe's government under Prime Minister Adolphe Thiers, is a calculated attempt to co-opt Napoleonic nostalgia for the Orleanist regime. The ceremony draws enormous crowds and rekindles popular fervour for the Napoleonic legend. Ironically, this resurgent Bonapartism ultimately benefits not the Orleanist monarchy but Louis-Napoléon, imprisoned at Ham, who would ride it to power in 1848.
key_figures: Napoleon I (deceased, remains returned)
King Louis-Philippe (authorised the return)
Adolphe Thiers (Prime Minister, initiated the repatriation)
François d'Orléans, Prince de Joinville (commanded the Belle-Poule)

sources.name: Official French government records of the ceremony; accounts published in Le Moniteur Universel, December 1840
Michael Paul Driskel, As Befits a Legend: Building a Tomb for Napoleon (1993)

events.certainty_note: Massively public event documented in official and journalistic sources. Exact date undisputed.

events.title: Abdication of King William I of the Netherlands
events.description: On 7 October 1840, King William I of the Netherlands abdicates in favour of his son, who becomes King William II. William I's reign had been marked by the loss of Belgium following the 1830 revolution, and his stubborn refusal to recognise Belgian independence until the 1839 Treaty of London. His desire to marry a Catholic Belgian countess, which met fierce opposition from Protestant Dutch opinion, and his increasingly autocratic governance style contributed to his decision to step down. The abdication coincides with a constitutional revision that limits royal prerogatives.
key_figures: William I (abdicating King of the Netherlands)
William II (successor King of the Netherlands)

sources.name: Royal decree of abdication, 7 October 1840; Dutch Staatsblad
Jeroen Koch, Koning Willem I, 1772–1843 (2013)

events.certainty_note: Official constitutional act with exact date. No uncertainty.

events.title: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon publishes What is Property?
events.description: In 1840, Pierre-Joseph Proudhon publishes Qu'est-ce que la propriété? (What is Property?), containing his famous declaration 'Property is theft!' The work is one of the foundational texts of anarchist political philosophy, arguing that the right of property as conceived by bourgeois law is inherently exploitative because it allows owners to extract surplus value from workers. Proudhon distinguishes between 'property' (exploitative ownership of the means of production) and 'possession' (legitimate use). The book influences Karl Marx (who initially praises it before later attacking Proudhon's ideas in The Poverty of Philosophy, 1847), Mikhail Bakunin, and the broader European socialist and anarchist movements.
key_figures: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon (political philosopher)

sources.name: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon, Qu'est-ce que la propriété? (1840, first edition)
George Woodcock, Pierre-Joseph Proudhon: A Biography (1956)

events.certainty_note: Published work with known date. First edition copies survive. No uncertainty.

events.title: Lermontov publishes A Hero of Our Time
events.description: In 1840, Mikhail Lermontov publishes Geroy nashego vremeni (A Hero of Our Time) as a complete novel, after earlier serialisation of individual chapters. The novel, centred on the disenchanted officer Pechorin in the Caucasus, is widely considered the first major Russian prose novel in the psychological realist tradition. Its portrait of a 'superfluous man' — intelligent, capable, but morally adrift and destructive — defines an archetype that pervades Russian literature through Turgenev, Dostoevsky, and beyond. Lermontov writes from direct experience of military service in the Caucasus during Russia's ongoing colonial wars against Chechen and Circassian resistance.
key_figures: Mikhail Lermontov (author, army officer)
Tsar Nicholas I (reportedly disliked the novel's protagonist)

sources.name: Mikhail Lermontov, Geroy nashego vremeni (1840, first complete edition, Saint Petersburg)
Vissarion Belinsky, contemporary reviews in Otechestvennye Zapiski, 1840
Laurence Kelly, Lermontov: Tragedy in the Caucasus (1977)

events.certainty_note: Published work with known date. Contemporary reviews survive. No uncertainty.

events.title: Bombardment of Acre — British and Ottoman forces defeat Muhammad Ali
events.description: On 3 November 1840, a combined British, Austrian, and Ottoman naval squadron bombards the fortress city of Acre (Akka) in Ottoman Syria, held by Egyptian forces loyal to Muhammad Ali. The bombardment triggers a massive explosion in the Egyptian ammunition magazine, devastating the garrison and leading to the city's fall within hours. The destruction of Acre, combined with a British-supported Druze and Maronite uprising in Mount Lebanon and the earlier bombardment of Beirut in September, forces Muhammad Ali's son Ibrahim Pasha to evacuate Syria entirely. The campaign decisively ends Muhammad Ali's bid for an independent empire stretching from Egypt to Anatolia.
key_figures: Commodore Charles Napier (British naval commander)
Ibrahim Pasha (Egyptian commander in Syria, son of Muhammad Ali)
Muhammad Ali (Wali of Egypt)
Sultan Abdülmecid I (Ottoman Sultan)

sources.name: British Admiralty records; dispatches from Commodore Napier
Ottoman imperial firman and military correspondence
M.S. Anderson, The Eastern Question 1774–1923 (1966)

events.certainty_note: Military action documented in multiple national archives. Exact date well-established.

events.title: Transportation of convicts to New South Wales abolished
events.description: In 1840, the British government announces the end of convict transportation to New South Wales, following years of agitation by free settlers and the recommendations of the Molesworth Committee (1838). The last convict transport ship to Sydney arrives in November 1840. The decision reflects the growing political power of free settlers who view transportation as depressing wages and tainting the colony's reputation. Transportation continues to Van Diemen's Land (Tasmania) until 1853 and to Western Australia until 1868. The end of transportation to New South Wales accelerates the colony's transition to a free labour economy and self-governance.
key_figures: Lord John Russell (Colonial Secretary)
Sir William Molesworth (chairman of the parliamentary committee on transportation)

sources.name: Colonial Office dispatches, 1840; Order in Council ending transportation to NSW
Report of the Molesworth Committee on Transportation (1838)
Robert Hughes, The Fatal Shore (1987)

events.certainty_note: Official policy decision documented in Colonial Office records. The last transport ship's arrival is precisely dated.

events.title: Charles Wilkes claims discovery of Antarctic continent
events.description: In January–February 1840, the United States Exploring Expedition under Lieutenant Charles Wilkes sights and charts a long stretch of the Antarctic coastline, claiming discovery of an Antarctic continent. Wilkes's expedition operates simultaneously with French (Dumont d'Urville) and British (James Clark Ross) expeditions, all converging on Antarctica. Dumont d'Urville sights land on 19 January, and Wilkes claims sightings beginning 16 January, creating a contentious priority dispute. The segment of Antarctica charted by Wilkes is subsequently named Wilkes Land. The expedition is part of a broader six-vessel, four-year American scientific expedition (1838–1842) that also surveys Pacific islands and the Pacific Northwest coast.
key_figures: Lieutenant Charles Wilkes (U.S. Navy, expedition commander)
Jules Dumont d'Urville (French naval officer, rival Antarctic expedition)
Captain James Clark Ross (British Royal Navy, rival Antarctic expedition)

sources.name: Charles Wilkes, Narrative of the United States Exploring Expedition (1845)
Ship logs from USS Vincennes and USS Peacock, January–February 1840
Nathaniel Philbrick, Sea of Glory: America's Voyage of Discovery, the U.S. Exploring Expedition, 1838–1842 (2003)

events.certainty_note: The sightings are documented in ship logs, though exact dates and what exactly Wilkes saw on his earliest claimed dates remain disputed. Priority claims between Wilkes and d'Urville are still debated.

events.title: Dumont d'Urville claims Adélie Land for France
events.description: On 21 January 1840, French naval officer Jules Dumont d'Urville, commanding the corvettes Astrolabe and Zélée, lands on an islet off the Antarctic coast and claims the territory for France, naming it Terre Adélie (Adélie Land) after his wife. The French expedition had departed Hobart, Tasmania in January and navigated through pack ice to reach the coast. D'Urville's Antarctic claim, while largely symbolic given the continent's inaccessibility, becomes the basis for France's ongoing Antarctic territorial claim maintained through the present day under the Antarctic Treaty System.
key_figures: Jules Dumont d'Urville (French naval officer and explorer)

sources.name: Jules Dumont d'Urville, Voyage au Pôle Sud et dans l'Océanie (1841–1854)
Edward Duyker, Dumont d'Urville: Explorer and Polymath (2014)

events.certainty_note: Documented in expedition records and French naval archives. Date well-established.

events.title: Kamehameha III promulgates Hawaii's first constitution
events.description: On 8 October 1840, Kamehameha III promulgates the first written constitution of the Hawaiian Kingdom, transforming it from an absolute monarchy into a constitutional one. The constitution, drafted with significant influence from American Protestant missionaries (particularly William Richards and Gerrit P. Judd), establishes a bicameral legislature, a bill of rights, and formal property rights. It represents a deliberate effort by Kamehameha III to position Hawaii as a recognised sovereign state under international law, thereby forestalling annexation by Britain, France, or the United States. The constitution balances traditional Hawaiian chiefly authority with Western legal concepts, though its long-term effect is to facilitate the increasing power of Western residents in Hawaiian governance.
key_figures: Kamehameha III (King of Hawaii)
William Richards (American missionary and royal adviser)
Gerrit P. Judd (American missionary, later Hawaiian government minister)

sources.name: Constitution of the Hawaiian Kingdom, 8 October 1840 (published text)
Ralph S. Kuykendall, The Hawaiian Kingdom, Volume 1: Foundation and Transformation, 1778–1854 (1938)

events.certainty_note: Constitutional text survives. Date and content well-documented.

events.title: ండ Sack of Gondar and instability in Ethiopia during Zemene Mesafint
events.description: In the early 1840s, Ethiopia remains in the Zemene Mesafint ('Era of the Princes'), a period of fragmented power in which regional warlords (ras) dominate the figurehead Solomonic emperor. Around 1840, the ancient capital Gondar continues to decline after repeated sackings by rival lords. Ras Ali II of Yejju controls the emperor Yohannes III as a puppet, while other regional lords — including Wube Haile Maryam of Tigray and the rising Kassa Hailu (future Emperor Tewodros II) — compete for supremacy. Detailed chronology for specific months in 1840 is poorly attested in Western sources, though Ethiopian royal chronicles (particularly the chronicles of the emperors) provide some documentation.
key_figures: Ras Ali II of Yejju (dominant warlord)
Emperor Yohannes III (figurehead Solomonic emperor)
Wube Haile Maryam (Ras of Tigray)

sources.name: Ethiopian royal chronicles (compiled in Ge'ez, partially translated in the 19th and 20th centuries)
Sven Rubenson, The Survival of Ethiopian Independence (1976)

events.certainty_note: The Zemene Mesafint period is well-attested, but precise dating of individual events to specific months in 1840 is uncertain in available sources. The broader political situation described here spans the early 1840s.

events.title: Sayyid Said consolidates Zanzibar as centre of East African trade
events.description: By 1840, Sultan Sayyid Said of Oman has effectively transferred his capital from Muscat to Zanzibar, making the island the commercial hub of the western Indian Ocean. Zanzibar dominates the East African ivory and slave trades, with trading caravans penetrating deep into the interior to Lake Tanganyika and beyond. Clove plantations, worked by enslaved people, become a major export industry. Sayyid Said encourages Indian merchant communities (particularly Gujarati and Kutchi traders) to settle in Zanzibar, creating a cosmopolitan commercial entrepôt. The Sultanate's trade network shapes the economic and demographic landscape of the entire East African coast and interior, establishing Swahili as a regional lingua franca.
key_figures: Sayyid Said bin Sultan (Sultan of Oman and Zanzibar)

sources.name: British consular reports from Zanzibar (established 1841, but earlier trade observations exist)
Abdul Sheriff, Slaves, Spices and Ivory in Zanzibar (1987)
R. Coupland, East Africa and Its Invaders (1938)

events.certainty_note: The transfer of Said's capital to Zanzibar is conventionally dated to around 1840, though it was a gradual process rather than a single event. The broader economic patterns are well-documented.

events.title: ండ First Afghan War — British garrison besieged in Kabul
events.description: Throughout 1840, the British military occupation of Afghanistan, begun in 1839, becomes increasingly untenable. The British garrison in Kabul, along with Shah Shuja (the puppet ruler installed by the British), faces growing Afghan resistance. Dost Mohammad, the deposed emir, attempts to rally opposition from the north before surrendering to the British in November 1840 and being exiled to India. However, his surrender does not end resistance; tribal leaders and local commanders continue to resist the occupation, setting the stage for the catastrophic British retreat from Kabul in January 1842 in which nearly the entire force of 16,500 is destroyed.
key_figures: Dost Mohammad Khan (deposed Emir of Afghanistan)
Shah Shuja (British-installed ruler)
Sir William Hay Macnaghten (British Envoy to Kabul)
Major General Sir Robert Sale (British military commander)

sources.name: Sir John William Kaye, History of the War in Afghanistan (1851)
Dispatches of the British Indian government (India Office Records, British Library)
William Dalrymple, Return of a King: The Battle for Afghanistan, 1839–42 (2013)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in British military and diplomatic records. Dost Mohammad's surrender in November 1840 is precisely dated.

events.title: Samuel Morse receives US patent for the telegraph
events.description: On 20 June 1840, Samuel F.B. Morse receives US Patent No. 1,647 for his electromagnetic telegraph system, which transmits coded messages over electrical wires. While Morse is not the sole inventor of electrical telegraphy — William Fothergill Cooke and Charles Wheatstone had patented a telegraph in Britain in 1837, and other inventors were working on competing designs — Morse's system, using the simple dot-dash code that bears his name, proves the most commercially successful. However, Morse lacks federal funding at this point; it is not until 1844 that Congress appropriates $30,000 for a demonstration line between Washington and Baltimore, producing the famous 'What hath God wrought' message.
key_figures: Samuel F.B. Morse (inventor and painter)
Alfred Vail (collaborator and engineer)
Leonard Gale (chemistry professor, collaborator)

sources.name: US Patent No. 1,647, 20 June 1840 (US Patent Office records)
Kenneth Silverman, Lightning Man: The Accursed Life of Samuel F.B. Morse (2003)

events.certainty_note: Patent records provide exact date. No uncertainty about the patent, though priority disputes with other telegraph inventors are ongoing historical debates.

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above have contested or more nuanced dimensions than popular accounts suggest. (1) The Treaty of Waitangi (1840-002): The claim that the treaty established legitimate British sovereignty is challenged by Māori scholars and the Waitangi Tribunal, who argue that the Māori signatories could not have understood themselves as ceding sovereignty given the mistranslation of key terms. Some chiefs signed under coercion or without full understanding. Moreover, a significant number of prominent chiefs never signed at all. (2) Wilkes's Antarctic discovery claim (1840-018): Wilkes's earliest claimed sighting dates are disputed; James Clark Ross later argued that some of what Wilkes charted as land was actually open ocean, and the exact date of first sighting has been challenged by both French and British partisans. Modern scholarship tends to acknowledge multiple near-simultaneous sightings rather than awarding priority to any single expedition. (3) The Penny Black and postal reform (1840-004): While Rowland Hill is conventionally credited as the architect of penny postage, recent scholarship (e.g., Daunton, Royal Mail, 1985) has argued that Hill's role has been exaggerated and that many of his ideas were already circulating in Post Office reform discussions. (4) Proudhon's 'Property is theft' (1840-014): While widely cited as foundational to anarchism, scholars debate whether Proudhon's actual political philosophy is coherent, noting internal contradictions between his critique of property and his defence of small-scale private possession. Marx's later critique in The Poverty of Philosophy (1847) argues Proudhon's economics are fundamentally confused. (5) The characterisation of the Independent Treasury Act (1840-009) as sound fiscal policy is contested — critics then and now argue it contributed to deflationary pressures by locking up specie in government vaults.
historiographic_note: The year 1840 falls within one of the best-documented periods in global history, with rich archival sources in multiple languages. However, the surviving record is heavily biased toward European imperial perspectives — the Opium War, for instance, was long narrated primarily through British sources, with Chinese perspectives receiving serious scholarly attention only from the mid-20th century. Events in Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are mediated almost entirely through European observers, with indigenous perspectives (e.g., Māori oral traditions, Ethiopian chronicles) requiring specialist linguistic and cultural knowledge to access. The political and intellectual history of Europe and the United States dominates the record, while the lives of ordinary people, women, enslaved populations, and non-elite actors are far less visible. Colonial-era sources frequently carry assumptions of European civilisational superiority that must be read critically.

graph_edges.note: Harrison's election leads directly to his inauguration and death, establishing the Tyler precedent
Treaty of Waitangi disputes lead to Hone Heke's war and the Northern War of 1845
British operations in 1840 escalate into the decisive campaigns of 1841–42, ending with the Treaty of Nanking
Convention of London's terms enforced by the bombardment of Acre in November
The Act of Union's political tensions contribute to the drive toward Canadian Confederation
Exclusion of women from the Anti-Slavery Convention galvanises the women's rights movement leading to Seneca Falls
Louis-Napoléon's imprisonment and the Bonapartist legend set the stage for his 1848 election as President
The Retour des Cendres and Louis-Napoléon's Boulogne coup both reflect and feed Bonapartist sentiment in 1840 France
Proudhon's anarchist philosophy contributes to the intellectual ferment behind the 1848 revolutions
The deteriorating British position in Kabul through 1840 leads to the catastrophic retreat of January 1842
Morse's 1840 patent leads to the 1844 congressional demonstration line and the telegraph revolution
American and French Antarctic expeditions operate simultaneously in January 1840, creating priority disputes
